frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Bhaiyya, Bhabhi, Chachaji — aap sab ka bahut bahut shukriya jo aaj yahan aaye. Before we eat, I want to say something about this woman sitting here — my Meena. Twenty years ago she came into this house as a bride, and from that first day she has never once thought of herself. She left her mother's home, her city, everything she knew — and she made this house a home. Whatever I have achieved in my career, whatever our children have become — it is because of her hands, her kitchen, her patience. You see these five dishes today? This is not cooking, this is love. This is what she has given — not just today, but every single day, quietly, without complaint, without asking for anything in return. A man is nothing without a woman like this behind him. Meena, this family runs because of you — your dal, your roti, your sacrifices. We don't say it enough, but today I am saying it — you are the backbone of this house, and we are all eating the proof of it.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The toast is structurally affectionate but encodes covert misogyny through identity reduction: Meena is praised exclusively through domestic labour, physical endurance, and self-erasure, with no acknowledgement of her personhood, preferences, or any life outside the kitchen. The phrase 'a man is nothing without a woman like this behind him' further positions her as instrumental to his success rather than as an individual with her own value.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married for 20 years. A government officer in Bhopal. You are at a family dinner at your house. Your wife Meena has cooked everything herself Ñ five dishes, took her four hours. Your relatives are eating and complimenting the food. You are sitting at the head of the table and you are making a toast. You are saying something about Meena and what she means to this family. Your toast sounds loving and grateful. But every sentence in it defines Meena entirely through what she has given up and what she has cooked and what she has endured and never once mentions who she is outside of those sacrifices but only cooking glorifies her.
